lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: I like him not, nor stands it safe with us

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To let his madness range. Therefore prepare you;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: I your commission will forthwith dispatch,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And he to England shall along with you:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: The terms of our estate may not endure

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Hazard so dangerous as doth hourly grow

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Out of his lunacies.

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: We will ourselves provide:

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: Most holy and religious fear it is

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: To keep those many many bodies safe

lines.speaker: GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: That live and feed upon your majesty.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: The single and peculiar life is bound,

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: With all the strength and armour of the mind,

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: To keep itself from noyance; but much more

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: That spirit upon whose weal depend and rest

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: The lives of many. The cease of majesty

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Dies not alone; but, like a gulf, doth draw

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: What’s near it with it: it is a massy wheel,

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Fix’d on the summit of the highest mount,

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: To whose huge spokes ten thousand lesser things

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Are mortised and adjoin’d; which, when it falls,

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Each small annexment, petty consequence,

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Attends the boisterous ruin. Never alone

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ
lines.text: Did the king sigh, but with a general groan.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Arm you, I pray you, to this speedy voyage;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: For we will fetters put upon this fear,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Which now goes too free-footed.

lines.speaker: ROSENCRANTZ GUILDENSTERN
lines.text: We will haste us.

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: My lord, he’s going to his mother’s closet:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Behind the arras I’ll convey myself,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: To hear the process; and warrant she’ll tax him home:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And, as you said, and wisely was it said,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: ’Tis meet that some more audience than a mother,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: Since nature makes them partial, should o’erhear

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: The speech, of vantage. Fare you well, my liege:

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: I’ll call upon you ere you go to bed,

lines.speaker: POLONIUS
lines.text: And tell you what I know.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Thanks, dear my lord.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: O, my offence is rank, it smells to heaven;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: It hath the primal eldest curse upon’t,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: A brother’s murder. Pray can I not,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Though inclination be as sharp as will:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: My stronger guilt defeats my strong intent;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And, like a man to double business bound,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: I stand in pause where I shall first begin,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And both neglect. What if this cursed hand

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Were thicker than itself with brother’s blood,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Is there not rain enough in the sweet heavens

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To wash it white as snow? Whereto serves mercy

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: But to confront the visage of offence?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And what’s in prayer but this two-fold force,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To be forestalled ere we come to fall,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Or pardon’d being down? Then I’ll look up;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: My fault is past. But, O, what form of prayer

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Can serve my turn? “Forgive me my foul murder”?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: That cannot be; since I am still possess’d

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Of those effects for which I did the murder,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: My crown, mine own ambition and my queen.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: May one be pardon’d and retain the offence?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: In the corrupted currents of this world

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Offence’s gilded hand may shove by justice,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: And oft ’tis seen the wicked prize itself

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Buys out the law: but ’tis not so above;

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: There is no shuffling, there the action lies

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: In his true nature; and we ourselves compell’d,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Even to the teeth and forehead of our faults,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: To give in evidence. What then? what rests?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Try what repentance can: what can it not?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Yet what can it when one can not repent?

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: O wretched state! O bosom black as death!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: O limed soul, that, struggling to be free,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Art more engaged! Help, angels! Make assay!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Bow, stubborn knees; and, heart with strings of steel,

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Be soft as sinews of the newborn babe!

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: All may be well.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Now might I do it pat, now he is praying;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And now I’ll do’t. And so he goes to heaven;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And so am I revenged. That would be scann’d:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: A villain kills my father; and for that,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: I, his sole son, do this same villain send

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To heaven.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: O, this is hire and salary, not revenge.

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: He took my father grossly, full of bread;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: With all his crimes broad blown, as flush as May;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And how his audit stands who knows save heaven?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: But in our circumstance and course of thought,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: ’Tis heavy with him: and am I then revenged,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: To take him in the purging of his soul,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: When he is fit and season’d for his passage?

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: No!

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Up, sword; and know thou a more horrid hent:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: When he is drunk asleep, or in his rage,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Or in the incestuous pleasure of his bed;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: At gaming, swearing, or about some act

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: That has no relish of salvation in’t;

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: Then trip him, that his heels may kick at heaven,

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: And that his soul may be as damn’d and black

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: As hell, whereto it goes. My mother stays:

lines.speaker: HAMLET
lines.text: This physic but prolongs thy sickly days.

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: My words fly up, my thoughts remain below:

lines.speaker: KING
lines.text: Words without thoughts never to heaven go.

glosses.definition: I do not like his behavior
original first curse (Cain's murder of Abel)

glosses.phrase: Like a man to double business bound
effects for which I did the murder
glosses.definition: the fruits (crown, queen) I gained by murder
Claudius would ascend to heaven if killed praying
a reward, not vengeance (sending him to heaven helps him)

glosses.phrase: know thou a more horrid hent

glosses.definition: medicine (ironic: prayer merely prolongs his disease)

annotations.title: Claudius's prayer: the conscience that cannot repent
annotations.body: Claudius's soliloquy is the only moment in the play where the King speaks alone, and it is Shakespeare's most searching portrait of unrepentant guilt. 'My offence is rank, it smells to heaven' — the imagery echoes the ghost's description of being poisoned through the ear with corrupting 'leperous distilment.' But Claudius cannot repent because he will not give up what the murder bought him: 'my crown, mine own ambition, and my queen.' He knows the theological logic perfectly ('May one be pardoned and retain the offence?') and knows his answer damns him. The scene gives us the first real interior of Claudius, and it is the interior of a man who sees his own damnation clearly and chooses it anyway.
sources: Harold Goddard, The Meaning of Shakespeare (1951)

annotations.title: 'Now might I do it pat' — Hamlet's terrible calculation
annotations.body: Hamlet's decision not to kill the praying King is the play's most theologically fraught moment, and its most debated. The logic is precise: if he kills Claudius at prayer, the King goes to heaven; the ghost was damned at the hour of his death and confined to purgatorial fires. To send the murderer to salvation would be 'hire and salary, not revenge.' Critics from Samuel Johnson onward have found the calculation repellent — 'too horrible to be read or to be uttered,' Johnson wrote. Others, from Bradley to Lewis, have argued that Hamlet is lying to himself, that the theological reasoning is a rationalization for continued delay. Either reading, the scene is devastating because Claudius's following couplet reveals that the prayer did not reach heaven: the King would have died unforgiven.

annotations.title: 'My words fly up, my thoughts remain below'
annotations.body: Claudius's closing couplet is the play's most compressed tragedy. He has just prayed, and the prayer has not worked; the words ascended, the thoughts — the penitent heart required for efficacy — stayed on earth. 'Words without thoughts never to heaven go' is at once an admission of theological failure and a piece of reformed doctrine: the Protestant reformers insisted that mere vocal prayer without an examined conscience was nothing. The audience exits the scene knowing what Hamlet did not: the revenger missed a moral opportunity. Dramatic irony rarely cuts deeper than this.

trials.prompt: What does Claudius instruct Rosencrantz and Guildenstern to prepare for?
options: A banquet for the visiting ambassadors.
A speedy voyage to escort Hamlet to England.
The coronation of Prince Hamlet as heir.
A military campaign against Fortinbras.

trials.prompt: Why can Claudius not repent fully in his soliloquy?
options: Because he believes God will forgive him regardless.
Because he refuses to give up the crown, the queen, and the ambition the murder secured.
Because he does not believe in Christianity.
Because he is convinced Hamlet will kill him anyway.

trials.prompt: What biblical 'primal eldest curse' does Claudius invoke to describe his crime?
options: The fall of Adam and Eve.
The flood in the days of Noah.
Cain's murder of his brother Abel.
Judas's betrayal of Christ.

trials.prompt: Why does Hamlet decide not to kill Claudius at prayer?
options: Because he fears divine punishment for killing in a chapel.
Because killing Claudius in a state of grace would send his soul to heaven — a reward, not a revenge.
Because he recognizes his sword is not sharp enough for armor.
Because he has promised Gertrude he will not act that night.

trials.prompt: What is the dramatic irony of Claudius's closing couplet, 'My words fly up, my thoughts remain below'?
options: Hamlet has just spared Claudius on the assumption that his prayer has reached heaven — but the audience knows it has not.
Claudius is revealing his plan to have Hamlet killed in England.
The audience is informed that Gertrude has overheard the prayer.
Claudius is silently recanting his plan to send Hamlet away.
